Home building permits (decks, roofs, water heaters, basement finishes):  ·  Call PPRBD 719-327-2880  ·  pprbd.org →  ·  (separate agency — not this portal)

COS
City of Colorado Springs Permits, licenses & records
secure .gov

Plain-language routing

Tell us what you need.
We'll point you to the right place.

Describe your situation in your own words. You do not need to know which City department handles it. We'll match you to the correct service and give you a direct link.

→

No 31-option dropdowns. No "Contact Type" jargon. No guessing which tab to click. Just tell us what happened, and we handle the routing.

What do you need to do?

Describe it in your own words. One sentence is enough.

0 / 500

Be as specific as you can. Addresses, dates, and what you're trying to accomplish all help.

Takes about 3 seconds.
We'll give you a direct link.

Figuring out where this goes…

Suggested department

—

—

Not what you meant?

All departments & direct links

Most residents
PPRBD (separate site)
All residential building permits: decks, roofs, HVAC, plumbing, electrical, basement finishes. 719-327-2880.
Complaints & violations
Neighborhood Services
Tall grass, junk vehicles, nuisances, zoning violations, unpermitted short-term rentals.
Streets & events
Public Works
Right-of-way work, traffic control, special events, parades, block parties, street closures.
Starting a business
Business Licensing
City business licenses, renewals, changes to existing licenses.
Police reports
Police Records
Crash reports, background checks, clearance letters, public records requests.
Land use & zoning
Planning
Rezoning, subdivision, conditional use, plats. For development, not home remodels.
Commercial fire safety
Fire
Alarms, sprinklers, hazmat, commercial kitchens, liquor licenses, commercial plan review.
Developers & contractors
Stormwater
Erosion control, stormwater plans, outfall compliance. Mostly for construction sites.
Infrastructure work
City "Building" tab
Concrete, excavation, CSU utility permits. NOT residential — that's PPRBD.

The current Colorado Springs permit portal runs on Accela Citizen Access — a hybrid of 2008-era ASP.NET WebForms and a newer Angular layer. Its front door asks residents to pick from nine department tabs organized around the City's org chart, then forces them through a 31-option "Contact Type" dropdown with options like Fire - Billing - DO NOT CHANGE (an internal admin note leaked into public UI). The top-nav link colors fail WCAG contrast at 2.16:1, no focus indicator exists on form fields, and validation errors never announce to screen readers. This demo is a sketch of what the front door could look like: one plain-language textarea, AI routing to the right department, and a visible fallback grid if the suggestion is wrong.

Residents don't think in departments
A resident's intent is "my neighbor's grass is too tall" — not "I need the Enforcement module." The current portal makes the citizen do the translation. Plain-language routing moves that work to the system.
PPRBD is the biggest invisible fork
Most residents who want a permit want a residential building permit — which is PPRBD, a completely separate agency at pprbd.org. The current COS portal has a "Building" tab that suggests otherwise. Anyone adding a deck, replacing a water heater, or finishing a basement should be at PPRBD. Surfacing that branch at the top is the single highest-impact fix.
The "Contact Type" step is a user-hostile routing tax
Asking a resident to classify themselves as "Resident Agent for Service" or "Fire - Billing - DO NOT CHANGE" with no help text is WCAG 3.3.2 failure and a UX disaster. A routing system should infer contact type from parcel ownership, verified identity, and action — not ask.
Reversible identity creation
The current system commits your username to the database before email confirmation, creating orphan accounts that only staff can delete. This demo has no account creation — you land, ask, get a link. Login happens on the destination, only if needed.
Always-visible alternatives
The AI suggestion can be wrong. The reference grid below the result shows all nine paths at all times so a resident is never trapped in a bad routing decision. WCAG 2.4.5 — multiple ways to navigate.
Architectural note
This is a front-door prototype, not a full system. In the civic-Jira architecture this maps onto the "Public Layer" — plain-language output, property card, notifications — sitting above the Parcel Engine and the Record/Ticket system. The routing intelligence lives here; the actual records stay wherever they already live (Accela, PPRBD, or anywhere else).

City of Colorado Springs · Permit portal redesign — prototype
